findings: No input validation on category/slug parameters passed to where() queries - allows injection patterns
deleteProduct uses soft-delete (isActive=false) which is good, but orphaned data could accumulate
No rate limiting on getProducts() calls - potential for enumeration attacks
Client-side Firestore usage exposes query patterns to browser
fileClassifications.recommendation: Add parameter validation before query construction. Implement server-side rate limiting. Consider server-side queries for sensitive operations.

findings: JWT verification is good, but broad catch-all error handling masks debugging info
No logging of failed auth attempts - limits incident detection
Exposes 'authenticated: false' in both 401 and token-missing scenarios (minor timing attack surface)
fileClassifications.recommendation: Add structured logging for auth failures. Consider rate-limiting this endpoint. Ensure JWT expiry is short (currently 30 days is too long).

findings: Simple cookie deletion is appropriate
POST method correctly used for state-changing operation
Missing CSRF token validation (Next.js handles via same-site cookies, but explicit validation better)
fileClassifications.recommendation: Add optional CSRF token validation. Consider checking if token was actually set before deletion.

findings: Weak authorization: accepts Bearer token from Firebase but no server-side role verification. Any authenticated user can upload.
Content-type whitelist is good (jpeg/png/webp only), but no file size limit specified
No filename sanitization - using raw filename in S3 key construction could bypass restrictions
Missing multipart/form-data validation - relies on client enforcing type restrictions
Presigned URL TTL of 15 minutes is appropriate, but no download restrictions on generated URLs
No logging of who uploaded what - audit trail missing
fileClassifications.recommendation: CRITICAL: Implement server-side admin role check in Firebase Auth claims. Add max file size limit (suggest 5MB). Sanitize filenames in S3 key. Log all uploads with user ID and timestamp. Validate content-type server-side by reading file magic bytes.

findings: CRITICAL: No input validation on amount/currency/description - vulnerable to injection/manipulation
Missing amount validation: no min/max checks, could allow $0 or negative values
Description is URL-encoded WITHOUT sanitization - encodeURIComponent only prevents URL breakage, not injection
Mock payment flow exposes sensitive data in URL (amount, description visible in logs)
No CSRF protection on POST endpoint
Missing fraud detection: no user identification, no transaction limits
Commented code reveals unimplemented Bold API integration - suggests incomplete implementation
No PCI-DSS considerations mentioned - payment data flows through client
Currency not validated against allowed list
fileClassifications.recommendation: CRITICAL: Add Zod/Joi schema validation for amount (min: 100, max: 10000000), currency (whitelist), description (max 255 chars, no special chars). Implement server-side amount verification. Remove/secure commented code. Add user authentication check. Implement CSRF token. Do NOT handle card data client-side - use Bold's hosted payment forms. Add logging and fraud detection.

findings: Base64-encoded Firebase service account in env var is non-standard - adds decoding layer but doesn't improve security
Missing error handling for malformed base64 string could leak partial credentials in error message
No rotation mechanism for service account key
admin.ts is marked 'server-only' (good), but leaks detailed error on parse failure
fileClassifications.recommendation: Store service account as JSON directly in env (Firebase/Vercel handle secrets correctly). Add try/catch around parsing with generic error. Implement key rotation schedule (recommend quarterly). Add monitoring for service account usage patterns.

findings: AWS credentials in environment variables - standard practice but visible if env is compromised
S3 bucket name not validated - uses env var without fallback, could fail silently
No bucket policy enforcement on file types at S3 level - relies only on code validation
Presigned URL TTL (15 min) is good, but no option to customize per use case
Public URL generation assumes bucket is public - no validation of actual permissions
ContentType parameter passed from client (via upload route) - could be overridden
fileClassifications.recommendation: Use AWS IAM role-based authentication (preferred) instead of access key ID + secret. Set bucket policy to deny non-image uploads. Add S3 server-side encryption. Validate file content server-side before S3 upload (magic bytes). Use S3 object tags for audit tracking.

findings: HARDCODED PHONE NUMBERS in source code: '573025064629' and component has '3025064629' - massive security flaw
OTP stored in public Firestore collection 'admin_otps' - any authenticated user can enumerate/brute-force
OTP generation uses weak randomization: Math.random() is cryptographically insecure
OTP expiration check has inconsistent handling: .toDate() vs direct comparison
10-minute OTP expiry is industry standard but no max attempts limit implemented
userId is generated client-side (temp-${Date.now()}) - predictable and can be brute-forced
No rate limiting on verifyOTP - vulnerable to brute-force (6 digits = 1M combinations)
WhatsApp API token and phone stored in env but no validation of API responses
isAdminAuthenticated function does admin claim verification (good), but AdminLogin component doesn't enforce it
verifyPhoneAndSetCookie queries admin_users collection on client Firestore - data exposure
No logging of OTP verification attempts for audit trail
Phone number hardcoding prevents scaling to multiple admins
OTP code never cleared from localStorage after verification
fileClassifications.recommendation: CRITICAL ISSUES TO FIX: (1) Move phone numbers to backend-controlled allowlist in Firestore with access control. (2) Use crypto.getRandomValues() for OTP generation. (3) Move OTP verification to server-side route (no client Firestore). (4) Implement rate limiting on OTP requests (3 attempts, 15-min lockout). (5) Make userId server-generated and non-predictable. (6) Store OTP in private server-only collection with TTL deletion. (7) Implement audit logging for all auth events. (8) Remove hardcoded phone from components - fetch from secure config. (9) Add max-login-attempts tracking per phone.

findings: HARDCODED PHONE: '3025064629' duplicated in allowlist - same as cookies.ts
No rate limiting on request submission - user can spam OTP requests
Phone number input strips non-digits but no other validation (length, format)
OTP input accepts 6 digits but no validation on submission - relies on backend
Error messages are generic (good for not leaking info), but could be more specific for UX
useId not generated - state management relies on userId from server
fileClassifications.recommendation: Remove hardcoded phone array - fetch from server config endpoint. Implement client-side rate limiting (disable button for 60s after submission). Add phone format validation (should be 10 digits after +57). Validate OTP is exactly 6 digits before submission. Add exponential backoff UI feedback.

findings: Client-side auth check via /api/admin/check-auth - good for UX but not security
No Server Component middleware to block unauthenticated access - client can bypass check if JS fails
Auth redirect uses router.replace (good - prevents back button), but timing window exists
Loading state shows 'Verificando acceso...' but doesn't prevent route navigation during check
No timeout on fetch request - could hang indefinitely if API is slow
fileClassifications.recommendation: Use Next.js Server Component with server-side auth check (middleware or direct isAdminAuthenticated() call). Implement request timeout (5s). Add fallback to login page on network error. Consider moving auth check to middleware.ts.

findings: Client-side component calls getProducts() with no auth check - relies on upstream auth check
Search filter uses .toLowerCase().includes() - vulnerable to ReDoS with specially crafted input
Category filter accepts any string from CATEGORIES array - good, but no enum validation
Delete operation shows confirm dialog (good UX), but only client-side confirmation
Product edit link navigates to /admin/productos/{id}/edit - ID comes from database, safe
No pagination on initial load - could load 1000+ products into memory
No permission check on delete/restore operations - assumes all logged-in users are admins
fileClassifications.recommendation: Add server-side auth check before getProducts(). Implement efficient pagination with limit. Use regex or proper string matching instead of includes() for search. Add server-side confirmation of admin status before allowing edit/delete. Consider implementing optimistic updates.

findings: Uses Client Component but calls getToken() which requires Firebase client auth (OK)
Zod schema validates form fields (good), but missing several critical checks
slug field requires regex /^[a-z0-9-]+$/ - good, but no uniqueness validation
Image upload to S3 via presigned URL - proper flow, but auth uses Firebase token (not admin-verified)
uploadImageToS3 does minimal validation on response - could inject malicious URLs
No file type validation beyond MIME type check - could accept spoofed files
Gallery image upload in loop - no parallel upload protection, could be slow
temp-${Date.now()} product ID is predictable during form editing
fileClassifications.recommendation: Add server-side admin role check before upload. Validate slug uniqueness against Firestore before save. Implement file magic byte validation server-side. Add max file size limit to Zod schema. Add concurrent upload limit. Use UUIDs for temp product IDs. Validate S3 response signature.

findings: Searches only against hardcoded FEATURED_PRODUCTS array (not all products) - limited scope is good
No input sanitization but uses .toLowerCase().includes() which is safe from injection
Debounce of 300ms is appropriate
No rate limiting at component level, but local-only data means no backend impact
fileClassifications.recommendation: Consider expanding search to full product set if needed (would require server-side search). Keep debounce for performance. No critical issues here.

findings: Stores cart in localStorage without encryption - user can modify quantities/prices
No integrity check on localStorage data - JSON.parse fails silently on corruption
Prices stored in cart are used client-side - vulnerable to price manipulation before sending to payment
No cart expiry - old data persists indefinitely
Cart operations are local-only - no server-side validation of quantities/availability
fileClassifications.recommendation: IMPORTANT: Never trust client-side prices for payment. Payment API must fetch current prices from Firestore. Add cart validation on checkout (verify item exists, check stock, fetch current price). Consider cart expiry (e.g., 7 days). Store cart as immutable snapshots for audit trail.

findings: Uses Next.js Image component (safe from XSS)
Product name, price rendered directly but from typed Product object (safe)
Badge and discount calculations are numeric (safe from injection)
fileClassifications.recommendation: No critical issues. Continue using Next.js Image and typed props.

findings: Fetches product by slug from database (safe)
Product description rendered directly - could contain user-generated content (not currently, but risk exists)
Uses window.location.href for payment redirect - proper but no validation of URL
generateBoldPaymentLink returns URL without validation - could redirect to malicious URL if Bold API is compromised
Quantity input allows manual entry via number input - no min/max validation
fileClassifications.recommendation: Add HTML sanitization if product descriptions become user-editable. Validate payment URLs match Bold domain before redirect. Add quantity bounds to number input (min: 1, max: 100). Implement CSP header to restrict redirects.

findings: Uses NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix correctly for Firebase client SDK config - proper practice
No sensitive secrets in public config
Firebase security rules must enforce authorization (not visible in code review)
fileClassifications.recommendation: Ensure Firestore security rules restrict write access to admin-verified users. Review rules separately: /admin_users and /admin_otps collections should be admin-only, /products readable by all.

findings: Well-defined TypeScript interfaces reduce runtime errors
Optional fields use proper ? syntax
No security issues at type level
fileClassifications.recommendation: Keep strict TypeScript enabled. No issues here.

findings: Hardcoded featured products and categories are configuration, not sensitive
No secrets or PII
fileClassifications.recommendation: Consider moving FEATURED_PRODUCTS to Firestore for dynamic updates without deployment.

findings: Image remotePatterns whitelist is good - prevents arbitrary image loading
S3 hostname uses wildcard fallback (**.amazonaws.com) - too permissive, allows any AWS S3 bucket
No protocol restriction beyond https
No content-type validation at Next.js level (happens at browser)
fileClassifications.recommendation: Change S3 wildcard from '**.amazonaws.com' to specific bucket hostname (e.g., 'my-bucket.s3.amazonaws.com'). Remove unsplash.com and picsum.photos if not needed in production - they're external image sources.

findings: No input validation before sending to payment API - relying on /api/payments/bold validation
Throws generic 'Failed to generate...' error - doesn't distinguish between client errors and server errors
No retry logic - single failure = payment failure for user
Assumes paymentUrl always present in response - no null checks
fileClassifications.recommendation: Add Zod validation in this client (mirror of server validation). Implement exponential backoff retry (2-3 attempts). Add specific error types (BadRequest, ServerError, NetworkError). Validate URL is HTTPS before returning.

findings: All major dependencies are from reputable sources (Firebase, AWS SDK, Next.js)
jose library for JWT is industry-standard (good)
axios is fine but fetch is now standard (minor)
No lockfile version pinning visible (check pnpm-lock.yaml for integrity)
No known vulnerable packages detected in listed versions
fileClassifications.recommendation: Run 'pnpm audit' regularly. Consider using npm audit in CI/CD. Update to latest patch versions of Firebase and Next.js regularly. Use exact versions in package.json (no ^).

criticalIssues.title: Hardcoded Phone Numbers for Admin Authentication
criticalIssues.description: Phone numbers '573025064629' and '3025064629' are hardcoded in source code (cookies.ts and AdminLogin.tsx). This allows anyone with repo access to bypass OTP entirely and prevents adding new admin users without code changes.
criticalIssues.recommendation: Move phone allowlist to Firestore collection with admin-only write access. Fetch on server-side only. Use UUID-based admin user identifiers instead of phone numbers.

criticalIssues.title: Payment API Input Validation Missing
criticalIssues.description: No validation on amount, currency, or description in /api/payments/bold/route.ts. Attackers can manipulate payment amounts, inject special characters, or trigger parsing errors.
criticalIssues.impact: CRITICAL - Payment fraud and injection attacks
criticalIssues.recommendation: Implement Zod schema: amount (100-10000000 COP), currency (whitelist: 'COP'), description (max 255, no special chars). Validate server-side.

criticalIssues.title: OTP Brute-Force Vulnerability
criticalIssues.description: No rate limiting on verifyOTP endpoint. A 6-digit OTP has only 1 million combinations. Attacker can try all codes in seconds without detection.
criticalIssues.recommendation: Implement rate limiting: max 3 verification attempts per userId, 15-minute lockout after failure. Log all attempts. Use exponential backoff.

criticalIssues.title: Weak OTP Generation (Not Cryptographically Secure)
criticalIssues.description: OTP uses Math.random() which is predictable and not cryptographically secure. Attacker can predict OTP codes.
criticalIssues.impact: CRITICAL - OTP codes can be predicted
criticalIssues.recommendation: Use crypto.getRandomValues() for secure random number generation: `Math.floor(crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint32Array(1))[0] / 4294967295 * 900000) + 100000`

criticalIssues.title: Weak File Upload Authorization
criticalIssues.description: /api/admin/upload/route.ts allows ANY authenticated Firebase user to upload images. No role/admin verification. Attackers can upload malicious files.
criticalIssues.impact: CRITICAL - Unauthorized file uploads, potential code injection
criticalIssues.recommendation: Add Firebase custom claim verification: `if (!userRecord.customClaims?.admin) return 401`. Implement file magic byte validation. Add file size limits (max 5MB).

criticalIssues.title: OTP Stored in Public Firestore Collection
criticalIssues.description: OTP codes and phone numbers stored in 'admin_otps' collection without access control. Any authenticated user can enumerate and potentially brute-force OTPs.
criticalIssues.impact: HIGH - Information disclosure and OTP enumeration
criticalIssues.recommendation: Move OTP storage to server-only database (Firebase Admin, not client-accessible). Implement Firestore security rules to deny all read/write to non-admin users.

criticalIssues.title: Client-Side Price Storage (Cart)
criticalIssues.description: CartContext stores product prices in localStorage. Attacker can modify prices before checkout and send $0 payments.
criticalIssues.recommendation: Payment API must ALWAYS fetch current prices from Firestore. Never trust client prices. Validate on server-side before processing payment.

criticalIssues.title: OTP Collection Cleanup Missing
criticalIssues.description: OTP documents are not automatically deleted after expiry. Old OTP codes accumulate in Firestore, increasing storage costs and audit complexity.
criticalIssues.impact: HIGH - Data sprawl, potential privacy issue if old OTPs accessible
criticalIssues.recommendation: Implement Firestore TTL policy (set via admin SDK). Use Cloud Functions to clean up expired documents. Set TTL to 10 minutes (match OTP expiry).

summary: This Next.js e-commerce application has CRITICAL security vulnerabilities primarily in authentication and payment processing. The most severe issues are: (1) hardcoded phone numbers enabling authentication bypass, (2) missing input validation on payment amounts, (3) weak OTP generation and lack of rate limiting enabling brute-force attacks, (4) unauthorized file upload access, and (5) client-side price manipulation risk. The application has good foundational security practices (HTTPS, httpOnly cookies, Zod validation, Next.js Image component) but critical execution flaws in the auth system and payment flow. Immediate remediation is required before production deployment. Secondary concerns include ReDoS vulnerabilities in search, overly permissive S3 image hostname configuration, and missing audit logging. The cart system requires significant hardening to prevent payment fraud. Overall security posture: 3/10 (UNSAFE for production).

complianceNotes.dataProtection: Cart data in localStorage is unencrypted client-side storage - may violate GDPR if user data involved
complianceNotes.pciDss: NOT COMPLIANT - payment data flows through client, no PCI-DSS measures observed
complianceNotes.authentication: Weak - no MFA beyond OTP, no audit logging, hardcoded allowlists
complianceNotes.logging: Minimal - no audit trail for admin actions, auth failures, or payment events

nextSteps: IMMEDIATE: Move phone allowlist to Firestore with Firestore security rules
IMMEDIATE: Add Zod schema validation to /api/payments/bold with amount/currency/description bounds
IMMEDIATE: Implement rate limiting on OTP verification (3 attempts, 15-min lockout)
IMMEDIATE: Add Firebase admin role verification to /api/admin/upload
URGENT: Replace Math.random() with crypto.getRandomValues() for OTP generation
URGENT: Move OTP verification to server-side (remove from client-side Firestore queries)
URGENT: Ensure payment API fetches real-time prices from Firestore, validate server-side
HIGH: Implement Firestore security rules review (admin_users, admin_otps, products collections)
HIGH: Add audit logging for all admin actions, auth events, and payment processing
HIGH: Change S3 image wildcard from '**.amazonaws.com' to specific bucket hostname
MEDIUM: Implement file magic byte validation on S3 uploads
MEDIUM: Add structured error logging and monitoring for security events
MEDIUM: Review and restrict Next.js Image remotePatterns (remove unsplash.com, picsum.photos if not needed)
